questions.question: Why does OCR use CTC loss instead of plain cross-entropy?
options: CTC converges faster on GPU
Cross-entropy overflows for sequences longer than 32 characters
CTC is required by PyTorch's ONNX export path

OCR maps a fixed-length feature sequence to a variable-length character sequence without per-timestep alignment; CTC marginalises over all alignments that reduce to the target after merging repeats and removing blanks, so you can train on (image, text) pairs without knowing where each character starts in the image
questions.explanation: CTC (Graves et al., 2006) allows training sequence models when only the final label sequence is known, not the per-frame alignment. The model emits a distribution over (vocab + blank) at every time step; the loss sums over all paths that collapse to the target via merge-repeats-remove-blanks. This is why CRNNs can be trained on just text strings with no character bounding box annotations.

questions.question: In the classical OCR pipeline, after the text detector produces bounding boxes, what does the recognition stage output for each region?
options: Pixel coordinates of each individual character centroid within the region
A binary foreground/background mask for each word bounding box
A character sequence for each cropped region, produced by a CNN+BiLSTM+CTC that processes the fixed-height crop as a temporal sequence
A font-family classification used to select a downstream language model
questions.explanation: The recognition stage crops each detected box to a fixed height, runs a CNN to extract per-column features, passes those through a BiLSTM to model context, then applies CTC to produce a character sequence. No per-character timing labels are needed. PaddleOCR and EasyOCR both follow this CRNN+CTC architecture.

questions.question: In TinyCRNN.forward(), after the CNN produces features shaped (N, C, H', W'), how is the BiLSTM input prepared?
options: Global average pooling over all spatial dimensions to produce (N, C), then projected to hidden size
Average-pool the height dimension to 1 with f.mean(dim=2), then transpose to (N, W', C) so that W' becomes the time dimension fed to the BiLSTM
Flatten to (N, C×H'×W') and project down to the hidden dimension with a linear layer
Max-pool over width to collapse to (N, C, 1), then squeeze for the recurrent layer
questions.explanation: TinyCRNN's CNN is designed to reduce height to 1 via stacked MaxPool2d. f.mean(dim=2) finishes that collapse, giving (N, C, W'). Transposing to (N, W', C) puts width — the horizontal scan direction across the text — as the sequence dimension for the BiLSTM. This is the CRNN design: spatial features become a time series for the recurrent stage.

questions.question: Donut (Kim et al., 2022) skips the classical detect→recognise→layout pipeline entirely. What is its architecture?
options: A CNN feature extractor plus a conditional random field sequence decoder for structured output
Two BiLSTM encoders processing spatial and temporal features in parallel
PaddleOCR's DB text detector paired with a transformer-based recogniser
A ViT encoder that processes the full document image plus a text decoder that auto-regressively emits the target JSON, learning localisation and recognition implicitly through end-to-end training
questions.explanation: Donut has no explicit text detection or CTC recognition module. The ViT encoder sees the whole document; the transformer decoder emits the target string or JSON token-by-token. This eliminates error accumulation across pipeline stages but requires sufficient labelled (image, JSON) training pairs for the specific document type you are parsing.

questions.question: Why is greedy CTC decoding sometimes better in practice than beam search on clean printed-text datasets?
options: Greedy decoding is always superior to beam search for CTC models

When the model is confident at every time step — which is typical for clean printed fonts — greedy's per-step argmax is almost always the beam winner; beam search adds latency without meaningful CER improvement. Beam search helps on noisy or handwritten inputs where per-step probabilities are ambiguous
Beam search requires an external language model that is rarely available
Greedy always achieves lower character error rate than beam width 5
questions.explanation: Beam search only wins when per-step probabilities are uncertain. For clean printed text, the CRNN is highly confident at each column position. The greedy_ctc_decode in this lesson achieves within ~1% CER of beam width 5 on synthetic digit strings. Always benchmark both: the latency cost of beam search is real, and the accuracy gain depends entirely on input noise level.

questions.question: For a task measuring whether the model correctly extracted 'invoice_total: 42.50' from a receipt image, which evaluation metric is most appropriate — and why is CER insufficient?
options: CER (Character Error Rate) is sufficient because it measures every character in the output
WER (Word Error Rate) is sufficient because invoice fields are single words

F1 on structured fields is the right metric; it tests whether the correct semantic key-value pair appears in the output, not character-level transcription accuracy. CER would reward outputs that transcribe background text perfectly while missing the target field entirely
BLEU score, because it handles variable-length outputs robustly
questions.explanation: The three skill layers have different metrics. Raw OCR accuracy → CER/WER. Key-value extraction → F1 on structured fields ({item: price} pairs) or normalised tree edit distance on JSON (used in the Donut paper). CER is indifferent to semantic structure: a model that outputs every word correctly but in the wrong field mapping scores 100% CER but 0% field F1.
